Now I have all cities. Let me summarize the "abnormally hot" thresholds (the right dotted line marking where temperatures become abnormally hot):

- Atlanta: ~73-74°F (x-axis: 50°F to 80°F)

Phoenix stands out with the highest abnormally hot threshold at around 87-88°F, with Las Vegas coming in second at roughly 85-86°F. The remaining cities cluster in the 70-77°F range, except for San Francisco which is notably cooler at 57-58°F.

The dotted line marking the right edge of each chart shows the temperature threshold where historically rare conditions begin—where less than 1% of nights in the 1960s exceeded that point. This boundary separates the orange zone (where 2020s temperatures overlap with historical norms) from the red zone (abnormally hot nights in the 2020s).

The "abnormally hot" threshold is marked by the right dotted line in each panel — temperatures beyond which the 1960s distribution had essentially no nights (i.e., it's historically rare). Here are the approximate thresholds by city:

- **Phoenix**: ~87-88°F (x-axis extends to 100°F)
- **Las Vegas**: ~85-86°F (x-axis extends to 100°F)
- **New Orleans**: ~77°F
- **Houston**: ~77°F
- **Washington**: ~75°F
- **Atlanta**: ~73-74°F
- **New York (JFK)**: ~73-74°F
- **Chicago**: ~70°F
- **San Francisco**: ~57-58°F

Phoenix has the highest "abnormally hot" threshold, at approximately 87-88°F.
</think>

<answer>
Phoenix has the highest temperature threshold for "abnormally hot" temperatures, at approximately 87–88°F (its x-axis extends to 100°F, and the right dotted threshold line falls around 87–88°F, higher than any other city shown).
</answer>
